questions.text: As per the Motor Vehicles Act, which type of insurance is mandatory for all vehicles plying in public places in India?
options: Third Party Liability Insurance
Comprehensive Insurance
Own Damage Insurance
Personal Accident Cover for Passengers
questions.explanation: Third Party Liability insurance is a statutory requirement. It protects the vehicle owner against legal liability arising from death, bodily injury, or property damage caused to a third party.

questions.text: In Motor Insurance, the 'Insured Declared Value' (IDV) is technically defined as the:
options: Current market value of the vehicle including modifications.
The Sum Insured of the vehicle, calculated as the manufacturer’s listed selling price minus depreciation.
The price the owner paid when purchasing the vehicle.
The resale value of the car after an accident.
questions.explanation: IDV is the maximum amount the insurer will pay in case of a Total Loss. It is calculated by taking the manufacturer's listed price and applying a fixed depreciation percentage based on the age of the vehicle.

questions.text: What does a 'Personal Accident' (PA) policy primarily provide to the insured?
options: Reimbursement of routine hospital bills for fevers.
A monthly pension for life after retirement.
Fixed financial compensation for death or disability caused solely by an accident.
Coverage for the repair of the insured's vehicle.
questions.explanation: A PA policy is a benefit-based contract. It pays a pre-fixed sum if the insured suffers death or specified disabilities (Total or Partial) resulting from a sudden, visible, and external accident.

questions.text: Under a standard 'Householders' Insurance' policy, what is the core requirement for a 'Burglary' claim to be valid?
options: The items must be lost while the owner is traveling.
The theft must happen during the daytime.
The items must have been misplaced by the domestic help.
There must be evidence of 'forcible and violent' entry or exit from the premises.
questions.explanation: Burglary insurance covers theft only when it involves the use of force or violence to enter or exit a locked building. Simple theft (e.g., through an open door) is often excluded or covered under a separate clause.

questions.text: What happens to the 'No Claim Bonus' (NCB) in a Motor Insurance policy if a claim is made during the policy period?
options: The accumulated NCB is reduced to zero (lost) for the next renewal.
The NCB is doubled to help the customer pay for the accident.
The NCB remains unchanged if the accident was not the driver's fault.
The NCB is converted into a cash refund.
questions.explanation: NCB is a reward for safe driving and having a claim-free year. Even a small claim for a minor scratch will reset the accumulated NCB percentage to zero for the subsequent year.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of 'Permanent Total Disability' (PTD) in a Personal Accident policy?
options: A fracture in the arm that heals in 2 months.
Loss of sight in both eyes or loss of both hands/feet.
Loss of a single finger.
Temporary bed rest due to a minor back injury.
questions.explanation: PTD refers to an injury that permanently and totally prevents the person from engaging in any occupation. Loss of both eyes or two limbs is the standard definition of 100% PTD.

questions.text: In 'Travel Insurance', the 'Baggage Loss' cover typically applies to:
options: Items stolen from the traveler's home while they are away.
Loss of baggage while in the custody of the traveler at a hotel.
Total and permanent loss of 'checked-in' baggage by a common carrier (like an airline).
Slight damage to the suitcase handle during handling.
questions.explanation: Travel insurance specifically covers the total loss of baggage that has been checked in with the airline. It does not usually cover hand-carry items or minor wear and tear.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of a 'Shopkeepers' Package Policy' for a small business owner?
options: It provides free life insurance to all customers of the shop.
It guarantees that the shop's sales will increase.
It covers only the medical expenses of the shop owner.
It combines multiple covers (Fire, Burglary, Money-in-Transit, Liability) into a single, comprehensive policy.
questions.explanation: Package policies are designed for convenience. Instead of buying separate policies, a shopkeeper can protect their stock, furniture, and legal liabilities under one contract with a single premium.

questions.text: Which of the following is a permanent EXCLUSION in a standard Personal Accident insurance policy?
options: Death or injury resulting from attempted suicide or intentional self-injury.
Injury caused by a collision with another vehicle.
Slipping and falling in a bathroom.
A snake bite occurring during a trek.
questions.explanation: Insurance only covers fortuitous (accidental) events. Intentional acts like suicide or self-harm are fundamental exclusions in all insurance contracts.

questions.text: In Motor Insurance, 'Comprehensive' coverage refers to a policy that includes:
options: Only Third Party Liability.
Both Own Damage (OD) to the vehicle and Third Party Liability.
Life insurance for the owner's entire family.
Coverage for traffic fines and penalties.
questions.explanation: A Comprehensive policy (or Package policy) provides the widest protection by covering the mandatory Third Party requirements as well as damage to the insured's own vehicle.

questions.text: If an insured person under a Personal Accident policy is injured while driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, the claim will be:
options: Paid in full as it was an accident.
Paid with a 50% penalty.
Rejected entirely as it is a standard exclusion.
Paid only if the police do not file a case.
questions.explanation: Driving under the influence of intoxicants is an illegal act and a major moral hazard. It is a standard exclusion in almost all insurance policies, including Personal Accident and Motor.

questions.text: What does 'Liability' insurance protect a policyholder against?
options: Physical damage to their own property.
Natural disasters like floods and earthquakes.
The theft of their own personal belongings.
Legal obligations to pay compensation to third parties for injury, death, or property damage.
questions.explanation: Liability insurance is 'Other People's' insurance. It covers the legal costs and compensation the policyholder is required to pay when they are found at fault for harming someone else.

questions.text: In a 'Family Floater' health insurance policy, who does the Sum Insured cover?
options: The entire family together, where the sum can be used by any one or all members during the year.
Only the head of the family.
Each family member with a separate and equal individual limit.
Only the children until they reach age 18.
questions.explanation: A floater policy provides a pool of insurance. If the limit is 5 Lakhs, any member of the family can use it until the total 5 Lakhs is exhausted by the group.

questions.text: What is the significance of the '15-day Free Look Period' in retail insurance policies?
options: The customer can take a loan for 15 days.
The customer can review the policy terms and cancel for a refund if they disagree with the conditions.
The agent gets 15 days to collect the premium.
The insurer can cancel the policy without giving a reason.
questions.explanation: The Free Look period is a consumer protection feature. It allows the buyer to read the actual document and return it if the terms are not what they expected.

questions.text: In Motor Insurance, the 'No Claim Bonus' (NCB) can be transferred from:
options: One person to another when the vehicle is sold.
A father to a son for a new car purchase.
An old vehicle to a new vehicle purchased by the same owner.
A bike to a truck owned by the same person.
questions.explanation: NCB is a reward for the 'owner's' safe driving habits, not the vehicle. Therefore, the owner can carry their NCB discount from their old car to their new car.

questions.text: A Personal Accident policy usually covers 'Temporary Total Disability' (TTD). What benefit does this provide?
options: A lump-sum payment of the full Sum Insured.
Coverage for the hospital surgery bill.
Payment of the school fees for the insured's children.
A weekly allowance for a specified period to compensate for the loss of income while the insured is unable to work.
questions.explanation: TTD (like a leg fracture requiring 4 weeks of bed rest) prevents the person from working temporarily. The policy pays a weekly benefit (usually 1% of Sum Insured) to support them during that period.

questions.text: Which of the following would be covered under the 'Public Liability' section of a Shopkeepers' policy?
options: Legal compensation for a customer who slips on a wet floor in the shop and gets injured.
Theft of cash from the shop counter by an employee.
Damage to the shop's roof due to a heavy storm.
The shopkeeper's own medical treatment for a common cold.
questions.explanation: Public Liability covers injuries to 'third parties' (customers/visitors) that occur on the business premises due to the owner's negligence.

questions.text: What is the standard 'Deductible' or 'Excess' in a Motor Insurance claim?
options: The amount the agent deducts as their service fee.
The fixed initial portion of every claim that the policyholder must pay from their own pocket.
The discount given to the policyholder for being a safe driver.
The amount of tax paid on the claim payout.
questions.explanation: Deductibles ensure that the insured bears a small part of the loss, discouraging frivolous small claims and promoting better care of the vehicle.

questions.text: Overseas Travel Insurance is generally mandatory for which category of travelers?
options: Travelers going to another city within India.
Travelers going on a pilgrimage within their own state.
Travelers applying for a 'Schengen Visa' to visit European countries.
Travelers using their own private car for a trip.
questions.explanation: While recommended for everyone, many countries (especially Schengen nations) make travel insurance a compulsory requirement for the visa application process.

questions.text: In a Personal Accident policy, if the insured dies in an accident, the claim amount is paid to:
options: The Hospital management.
The Police department.
The Insurance Agent.
The Nominee or legal heirs of the insured.
questions.explanation: In the event of accidental death, the benefit (Sum Insured) is paid to the nominee designated by the policyholder to provide financial support to the family.

questions.text: Which document is required to prove that a vehicle is correctly insured for its Third Party Liability?
options: Certificate of Insurance
Manufacturer's manual
Driver's license
PUC (Pollution) certificate
questions.explanation: The Certificate of Insurance is the legal proof required by traffic authorities to verify that the vehicle complies with the compulsory insurance laws.

questions.text: What is covered under the 'Money-in-Transit' section of a Retail Business policy?
options: Loss of digital currency from a bank account.
Loss of business cash due to robbery while it is being carried between the shop and the bank.
Loss of profit due to a slow economy.
Loss of personal jewelry from the owner's home.
questions.explanation: Money-in-Transit specifically protects the physical cash of a business against theft or robbery while it is being moved by the owner or authorized employees.

questions.text: Why is 'Retail' insurance considered 'standardized'?
options: Because it is sold only in retail shopping malls.
Because it is designed only for rich corporate executives.
Because the policies have uniform terms and conditions to cater to a large number of individual customers easily.
Because the government sets the premium for every individual customer.
questions.explanation: Retail policies are mass-market products. They are standardized so that they can be sold quickly and efficiently to millions of individuals without needing complex, customized underwriting.

questions.text: Under a 'Householders' Package Policy', which of the following is usually NOT covered?
options: Damage to the building due to a fire.
Theft of electronic gadgets from the house.
Legal liability to domestic servants under the Workmen's Compensation Act.
Routine wear and tear or gradual depreciation of furniture.
questions.explanation: Insurance covers sudden and accidental losses. Gradual deterioration, rust, or normal wear and tear that happens over time is never covered by any general insurance policy.

questions.text: If a policyholder buys a 'Critical Illness' policy and is diagnosed with a covered major illness (like Kidney Failure), the company will pay:
options: Only the pharmacy bills.
The entire Sum Insured as a lump-sum payout, regardless of the actual treatment cost.
Double the amount of the agent's commission.
Nothing, until the person is hospitalized for 30 days.
questions.explanation: Critical Illness is a benefit-based retail product. It provides a large lump sum upon diagnosis to help the family manage lifestyle changes and non-medical costs associated with severe diseases.
